claims.claim_text: The module presents a July 2019 financial-services outage with a 14-hour duration and about $12 million in losses.
claims.anchor_text: In July 2019, a major financial services company experienced a 14-hour outage that cost them an estimated $12 million in lost transactions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A prolonged production outage at a payment-processing business can cause major operational and financial damage.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with fake-precise duration and loss figures.

claims.claim_text: The module gives precise timing for a memory leak reaching OutOfMemoryError after about six hours.
claims.anchor_text: The leak took roughly 6 hours to exhaust available memory, at which point the application began throwing `OutOfMemoryError` exceptions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A memory leak can gradually exhaust available memory until the application starts failing with out-of-memory errors.
claims.rationale: The exact duration is presented as historical fact without an attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module says the team missed the issue for another three hours because they only watched CPU.
claims.anchor_text: The operations team did not notice the issue for another 3 hours because they only monitored CPU utilization—the default CloudWatch metric for EC2.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If a team monitors only default EC2 metrics such as CPU utilization, a memory-driven failure can remain invisible for far too long.
claims.rationale: This is still part of the same unattributed incident and carries unsupported precise timing.

claims.claim_text: The module says cascading failures spread to three downstream payment services before the issue was investigated.
claims.anchor_text: By the time a customer complaint finally triggered a manual investigation, cascading failures had already spread to three downstream payment services.
claims.suggested_rewrite: By the time a manual investigation begins, failures can already have spread into downstream services.
claims.rationale: The named chain of events is presented as a real incident but is not attributable.

claims.claim_text: The module claims a specific preventive configuration would have alerted engineers six hours before the outage.
claims.anchor_text: Had they installed the CloudWatch Agent to collect memory and disk metrics, configured a custom metric for JVM heap usage, and set an alarm at 80% memory utilization, they would have received an automated alert 6 hours before the outage occurred.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Installing the CloudWatch Agent, publishing application-specific memory metrics, and alarming on rising memory pressure would have made the problem visible much earlier and enabled faster response.
claims.rationale: The exact counterfactual timeline is not citable as written.

claims.claim_text: The module estimates prevention would have cost about $3 per month in CloudWatch custom metrics.
claims.anchor_text: The total cost of prevention would have been roughly $3 per month in CloudWatch custom metrics.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The cost of publishing a small number of CloudWatch custom metrics is usually low compared with the cost of a production outage.
claims.rationale: The quoted dollar amount depends on unstated metric count and pricing assumptions.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch ingests over 1 trillion metrics per day.
claims.anchor_text: **CloudWatch ingests over 1 trillion metrics per day**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **CloudWatch operates at massive scale across AWS customers**
claims.rationale: I did not find a current AWS primary source that directly backs this exact daily figure.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch launched with the EC2 monitoring/auto-scaling/load-balancing wave in 2009.
claims.rationale: AWS's 2009 launch announcement directly supports the release timing.

claims.claim_text: EC2 basic monitoring publishes default metrics at 5-minute intervals and detailed monitoring publishes at 1-minute intervals.
claims.rationale: AWS CloudWatch docs explicitly describe EC2 basic versus detailed monitoring intervals.

claims.claim_text: The detailed-monitoring example prices EC2 detailed monitoring at about $2.10 per instance-month when 7 metrics are billed at $0.30 each.
claims.rationale: The AWS pricing page includes an EC2 detailed monitoring example with 7 metrics at $0.30 each.

claims.claim_text: Most production workloads strictly require 1-minute resolution to catch transient spikes.
claims.anchor_text: Most production workloads strictly require 1-minute resolution to catch transient spikes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Many production workloads benefit from 1-minute resolution when they need to catch short-lived spikes.
claims.rationale: This is a broad operational judgment stated too absolutely to support as a factual universal.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch Logs Insights can query terabytes of logs in seconds.
claims.anchor_text: **CloudWatch Logs Insights can query terabytes of logs in seconds**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **CloudWatch Logs Insights is designed for fast interactive analysis of large CloudWatch log datasets**
claims.rationale: AWS docs support fast interactive querying, but I did not find a passage that directly backs this exact scale-and-latency phrasing.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch Logs Insights was released in November 2018.
claims.rationale: The AWS launch announcement directly gives the November 2018 GA date.

claims.claim_text: Logs Insights has largely eliminated the need for external search clusters for ad-hoc querying.
claims.anchor_text: It was released in November 2018 and has largely eliminated the need for teams to ship logs to complex external search clusters just for ad-hoc querying.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It was released in November 2018 and gives teams a built-in way to run ad-hoc queries against logs already stored in CloudWatch.
claims.rationale: The elimination claim is a broad market judgment, not a verifiable product fact.

claims.claim_text: The CloudWatch Agent replaced three named older tools and those legacy tutorials are outdated.
claims.anchor_text: **The CloudWatch Agent replaced three older tools**: the CloudWatch Monitoring Scripts (Perl-based `mon-put-instance-data.pl`), the SSM CloudWatch Plugin (on Windows), and the older CloudWatch Logs Agent (`awslogs`). If you encounter legacy tutorials referencing these components, they are outdated.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The CloudWatch Agent is the current AWS-recommended agent** for collecting OS-level metrics and logs on EC2, and legacy tutorials based on older agent setups should be treated with caution.
claims.rationale: The exact replacement-history claim needs a stronger AWS migration source than I found.

claims.claim_text: Amazon ECS sends CPUUtilization and MemoryUtilization metrics to CloudWatch at one-minute intervals for running resources.
claims.span_hint: section: Other Services' Free Metrics table row: ECS
claims.rationale: The ECS metrics reference directly documents these metrics and the one-minute interval.

claims.claim_text: Application Load Balancer publishes RequestCount, TargetResponseTime, HTTPCode_Target_4XX_Count, and HealthyHostCount metrics at 60-second intervals when traffic is flowing.
claims.span_hint: section: Other Services' Free Metrics table row: ALB
claims.rationale: The ALB metrics reference names these metrics and documents 60-second publishing behavior.

claims.claim_text: Lambda emits Invocations, Duration, Errors, Throttles, and ConcurrentExecutions metrics with one-minute granularity.
claims.span_hint: section: Other Services' Free Metrics table row: Lambda
claims.rationale: The Lambda metrics docs describe these metric families and one-minute views/granularity.

claims.claim_text: DynamoDB aggregates ConsumedReadCapacityUnits, ConsumedWriteCapacityUnits, and ThrottledRequests at one-minute intervals.
claims.span_hint: section: Other Services' Free Metrics table row: DynamoDB
claims.rationale: The DynamoDB metrics reference explicitly lists these one-minute aggregated metrics.

claims.claim_text: EC2 default metrics do not include in-guest memory or filesystem space, so those signals require an in-instance agent.
claims.rationale: AWS docs state that the CloudWatch agent collects additional in-guest metrics such as memory and disk beyond EC2 defaults.

claims.claim_text: Custom metrics cost $0.30 per metric-month for the first 10,000 metrics, with lower tiers at higher volume.
claims.rationale: The AWS pricing page directly documents custom metric tier pricing.

claims.claim_text: A CloudWatch metric is uniquely identified by namespace, metric name, and dimensions.
claims.rationale: CloudWatch concepts docs explicitly define metric identity this way.

claims.claim_text: High-cardinality dimensions can create large numbers of custom metrics and unexpectedly high bills.
claims.rationale: AWS warns that high-cardinality dimensions create separate custom metrics and increase charges.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch can automatically extract metrics from structured JSON logs via Embedded Metric Format.
claims.rationale: The EMF docs directly describe automatic metric extraction from structured log events.

claims.claim_text: EMF is the most scalable way to publish custom metrics from Lambda functions and ECS tasks without blocking application threads.
claims.anchor_text: This is the Embedded Metric Format (EMF), and it is the most scalable way to publish custom metrics from Lambda functions and ECS tasks without blocking application threads.
claims.suggested_rewrite: This is the Embedded Metric Format (EMF), and it is a common way to publish custom metrics from Lambda functions and ECS tasks without separate `PutMetricData` calls in application code.
claims.rationale: The superlative claim is stronger than what the AWS docs directly state.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch metric alarms use the OK, ALARM, and INSUFFICIENT_DATA states.
claims.rationale: The alarm evaluation docs explicitly list these three states.

claims.claim_text: The treat-missing-data options are missing, notBreaching, breaching, and ignore, and the default behavior is missing.
claims.rationale: AWS documents both the option set and the default.

claims.claim_text: Composite alarms combine other alarms with Boolean logic to reduce noise and express more complex alert conditions.
claims.rationale: The composite alarm docs directly describe Boolean rule expressions and noise reduction.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch Logs retains data indefinitely by default.
claims.rationale: The CloudWatch Logs guide explicitly says log data is stored indefinitely by default.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch Logs retention values include the listed discrete day counts such as 1, 3, 5, 7, 14, 30, 60, and so on up to 3653.
claims.rationale: The PutRetentionPolicy API reference enumerates the allowed retention values.

claims.claim_text: The module says metric-filter evaluation is completely free.
claims.anchor_text: Crucially, evaluating logs with metric filters is completely free; you are not charged for the compute required to scan the incoming log streams.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Metric filters continuously evaluate incoming log streams and can turn matching patterns into CloudWatch custom metrics.
claims.rationale: AWS docs clearly price the resulting custom metrics, but I did not find a primary source that makes this exact no-compute-charge wording explicit.

claims.claim_text: Metrics extracted by metric filters are billed as CloudWatch custom metrics.
claims.rationale: The PutMetricFilter API reference explicitly warns that extracted metrics are charged as custom metrics.

claims.claim_text: The CloudWatch agent collects metrics, logs, and traces from EC2 and other environments.
claims.rationale: The agent install guide states this directly.

claims.claim_text: The CloudWatch agent configuration file has agent, metrics, logs, and traces sections.
claims.rationale: The configuration-file reference documents these top-level sections.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch agent config can be stored in Systems Manager Parameter Store and fetched with amazon-cloudwatch-agent-ctl using ssm:.
claims.span_hint: section: Storing Config in SSM and Starting the Agent
claims.rationale: AWS documents fetching agent config from Parameter Store with the ctl command.

claims.claim_text: The AWS managed policy CloudWatchAgentServerPolicy grants the core permissions needed for the CloudWatch agent, including PutMetricData, log writes, and GetParameter on AmazonCloudWatch-* parameters.
claims.rationale: The AWS managed policy reference includes the exact policy document.

claims.claim_text: CloudWatch alarm state changes are sent to EventBridge, which can route them to downstream targets.
claims.rationale: AWS explicitly documents CloudWatch alarm events flowing into EventBridge.

claims.claim_text: The cost table says Logs Insights has no free tier.
claims.span_hint: section: Cost Considerations and Best Practices table row: Logs Insights queries
claims.anchor_text: | Logs Insights queries | None free | $0.005/GB scanned |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Logs Insights queries | Included within the shared 5 GB CloudWatch Logs free tier | $0.005/GB scanned after the free tier |
claims.rationale: The current AWS pricing page includes Logs Insights scan volume inside the shared 5 GB CloudWatch Logs free tier.

further_reading.title: Collect metrics, logs, and traces using the CloudWatch agent
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the primary AWS guide for what the agent collects and where it runs.

further_reading.title: Analyzing log data with CloudWatch Logs Insights
further_reading.why_relevant: It covers Logs Insights capabilities, query behavior, and charging by queried data volume.

further_reading.title: Amazon CloudWatch Pricing
further_reading.why_relevant: Use this for current custom metric, alarm, dashboard, log ingestion, storage, and Logs Insights pricing.

notes: Quiz prompts and hands-on exercise steps were excluded per the task rules; duplicate factual material that reappears in quiz answers was not separately repeated when already captured from the main instructional sections.
